title: Christmas cake
subtitle: Rich British fruit cake, matured with brandy
excerpt: Christmas cake is a dense, dark fruit cake saturated with brandy and aged for weeks before marzipan and icing are applied. The cake bakes at a low temperature for three hours, then is fed with brandy weekly while stored. It improves with time. Most British households make it in October or early November.
sourceNotes: Rich fruit cake has been a feature of British celebration baking since at least the eighteenth century, when dried fruit, candied peel, and brandy became widely available. Earlier forms are recorded in Hannah Glasse's The Art of Cookery (1747). The association with Christmas solidified in the Victorian era. The tradition of feeding the baked cake with brandy at intervals before covering it with marzipan and icing is well documented in domestic cookery manuals from the mid-nineteenth century onward.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the undecorated, brandy-fed cake well wrapped for up to 6 months. Defrost thoroughly before applying marzipan and icing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make 4 to 8 weeks ahead and feed with 2 tablespoons of brandy every week until ready to decorate.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; wrap the outside of the tin with brown paper to prevent the edges burning; bake for 2 hours 45 minutes to 3 hours 15 minutes

content.text: The fruit should be soaked in brandy the night before baking: put all the dried fruit in a bowl with 3 tablespoons of brandy, cover, and leave overnight. The cake itself requires slow baking. Wrap the outside of the tin in a double layer of brown paper secured with string to protect the edges from the long time in the oven. A skewer through the centre should come out clean with no wet batter attached.

items.prepNote: for soaking, plus extra for feeding

content.text: Heat the oven to 140 degrees C fan. Grease a 20 cm round deep cake tin and line the base and sides with baking paper; bring the lining at least 5 cm above the rim. Wrap the outside of the tin in a double layer of brown paper. Beat 225 g softened butter and 225 g dark brown sugar together until combined (it will not cream fully given the dark sugar). Beat in 4 large eggs one at a time. Stir in 1 tablespoon of black treacle. Fold in 225 g plain flour sifted with 1.5 tsp mixed spice and half a teaspoon of cinnamon, then fold in 50 g ground almonds and all the soaked fruit with any brandy left in the bowl.

Spoon into the prepared tin and level the top with the back of a spoon. Make a slight hollow in the centre so the top bakes level. Bake for 2 hours 45 minutes to 3 hours 15 minutes. The cake is done when a skewer inserted through the centre comes out clean and the top is a deep mahogany. Leave to cool completely in the tin. Once cold, pierce all over with a skewer and spoon 2 tablespoons of brandy over the top. Wrap in baking paper and then foil. Repeat the brandy feeding once a week until you are ready to decorate.

items.problem: The outside is dark but the centre is still wet
items.solution: The oven was too hot. At 140 degrees C fan the cake should cook slowly and evenly; the brown paper insulation is essential. If this happens, reduce to 130 degrees C and continue baking, covered with foil on top.

items.problem: The fruit has sunk to the bottom
items.solution: The fruit was too wet (not drained after soaking) or the batter too thin. Pat soaked fruit with kitchen paper before folding in, and do not cream the butter and sugar to the pale, fluffy stage: a brief combination is correct for this batter.
